is an inner-city [suburb](/wiki/Suburbs_and_localities_(Australia) "Suburbs and localities (Australia)") in the [eastern suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia. Darlinghurst is located immediately east of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") (CBD) and [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney"), within the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of the [City of Sydney](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is often colloquially referred to as "Darlo".

**Darlinghurst** is an inner-city [suburb](/wiki/Suburbs_and_localities_(Australia) "Suburbs and localities (Australia)") in the [eastern suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") of [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia. Darlinghurst is located immediately east of the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district") (CBD) and [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney"), within the [local government area](/wiki/Local_government_in_Australia "Local government in Australia") of the [City of Sydney](/wiki/City_of_Sydney "City of Sydney").[[3]](#cite_note-3) It is often colloquially referred to as "Darlo".

Darlinghurst is a densely populated suburb with the majority of residents living in [apartments](/wiki/Apartment "Apartment") or [terraced](/wiki/Terraced_house "Terraced house") houses. Once a slum and [red-light district](/wiki/Red-light_district "Red-light district"), Darlinghurst has undergone [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") since the 1980s to become a cosmopolitan area made up of precincts. Places such as [Victoria Street](/wiki/Victoria_Street,_East_Sydney "Victoria Street, East Sydney") (which connects Darlinghurst to [Potts Point](/wiki/Potts_Point "Potts Point") in the north), [Stanley Street](/wiki/Stanley_Street,_East_Sydney "Stanley Street, East Sydney") (Little Italy) and [Crown Street](/wiki/Crown_Street,_Sydney "Crown Street, Sydney") (Vintage and Retro Fashion) are known as culturally rich destinations.[[4]](#cite_note-4) These [high street](/wiki/High_street "High street") areas are connected by a network of lane-ways and street corners with shops, cafes and bars.

Demographically, Darlinghurst is home to the highest percentage of [generation X](/wiki/Gen_X "Gen X") and [Y](/wiki/Gen_Y "Gen Y") in Australia.[[5]](#cite_note-5) The majority of businesses in Darlinghurst are independently owned and operated small businesses with over 50% of all commercial activity in the area being consumer oriented: indie retail, food, drink, dining, leisure and personal services.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Darlinghurst is also home to large number of off-street creative industries.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Darlinghurst's main street is [Oxford Street](/wiki/Oxford_Street,_Sydney "Oxford Street, Sydney"). This major Sydney road runs east from the south-eastern corner of [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney") through Darlinghurst and Paddington and terminates at [Bondi Junction](/wiki/Bondi_Junction "Bondi Junction"). Oxford Street is one of Sydney's most famous shopping and dining strips.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The Darlinghurst end is well known around the world as the centre of Sydney's [gay community](/wiki/Gay_community "Gay community"), is the yearly parade route of the [Sydney Mardi Gras](/wiki/Sydney_Mardi_Gras "Sydney Mardi Gras") and the spiritual birthplace of the [LGBTQ](/wiki/LGBTQ "LGBTQ") rights movement.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is home to a number of prominent gay venues and businesses, while more broadly Darlinghurst is a centre of Sydney's burgeoning small bar scene.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

From the 1990s onwards, Oxford Street began to garner a reputation for being Sydney's primary "[nightclub](/wiki/Nightclub "Nightclub") strip", popular with both [gay](/wiki/Gay "Gay") and [straight](/wiki/Heterosexuality "Heterosexuality") clubbers, surpassing the notorious [red-light district](/wiki/Red-light_district "Red-light district") of Kings Cross in popularity. As a result of the influx of revellers, crime rates increased in the area around 2007, particularly for [assaults](/wiki/Assault "Assault") and [robberies](/wiki/Robberies "Robberies").[[11]](#cite_note-11) This reported increase should be understood in terms of a very low background crime rate in East Sydney in general.[[12]](#cite_note-bocsar.nsw.gov.au-12) The 2014 [lockout laws](/wiki/Sydney_lockout_laws "Sydney lockout laws") saw many nightclubs close and the crime rate drop once again, with a new focus on small bars, restaurants and cafes after the lockout laws ended in 2020.

There are a number of named localities in and around Darlinghurst including [Taylor Square](/wiki/Taylor_Square "Taylor Square"), Three Saints Square,[[13]](#cite_note-13) and confusingly also [East Sydney](/wiki/East_Sydney_(locality) "East Sydney (locality)"). Locals have used this name to refer to the area immediately around Stanley Street in the suburb's west, however the title is used more broadly throughout the area from [Woolloomooloo](/wiki/Woolloomooloo "Woolloomooloo")[[14]](#cite_note-14) up to [Taylor Square](/wiki/Taylor_Square "Taylor Square")[[15]](#cite_note-15) where the old [Darlinghurst Gaol](/wiki/Darlinghurst_Gaol "Darlinghurst Gaol") still has the words East Sydney in brass lettering above the main entrance. This is because from 1900 to 1969 the entire area to the east of Sydney's CBD, from the harbour to [Redfern](/wiki/Redfern,_New_South_Wales "Redfern, New South Wales"), was an electorate known as the [Division of East Sydney](/wiki/Division_of_East_Sydney "Division of East Sydney"). Already in 1820 the entire ridge line running from Potts Point to Surry Hills was known as Eastern Hill.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Darlinghurst shares a postcode (2010) and an extensive soft southern border with neighbouring suburb [Surry Hills](/wiki/Surry_Hills "Surry Hills") which, with [Paddington](/wiki/Paddington,_New_South_Wales "Paddington, New South Wales") to the east and [Woolloomooloo](/wiki/Woolloomooloo "Woolloomooloo"), [Rushcutters Bay](/wiki/Rushcutters_Bay "Rushcutters Bay") and [Potts Point](/wiki/Potts_Point "Potts Point") to the north, comprise the metropolitan region of East Sydney. Although only minutes walk away from the Sydney CBD, this region is geographically distinct from it; separated from the more well known commercial centre by several landmarks: [Central railway station](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney"), [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney"), [St Mary's Cathedral](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Cathedral,_Sydney "St Mary's Cathedral, Sydney") and [The Domain](/wiki/The_Domain,_Sydney "The Domain, Sydney"). East Sydney hosts many well-known restaurants.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Sydney's [Eastern Suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") cover all the land from the east of Darlinghurst up to the [Pacific Ocean](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Taylor_Square%2C_Darlinghurst%2C_Sydney_NSW_-_2026.jpg/330px-Taylor_Square%2C_Darlinghurst%2C_Sydney_NSW_-_2026.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taylor_Square,_Darlinghurst,_Sydney_NSW_-_2026.jpg)

The southern end of [Taylor Square](/wiki/Taylor_Square "Taylor Square") adjacent to Oxford street. 2026.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Corner_of_Bourke_and_and_Campbell_street._Surry_Hills_and_Darlinghurst_-_March_2025_-_Daytime_-_panorama.jpg/330px-Corner_of_Bourke_and_and_Campbell_street._Surry_Hills_and_Darlinghurst_-_March_2025_-_Daytime_-_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Corner_of_Bourke_and_and_Campbell_street._Surry_Hills_and_Darlinghurst_-_March_2025_-_Daytime_-_panorama.jpg)

A [rainbow crossing](/wiki/Rainbow_crossing "Rainbow crossing") celebrating the area's LGBTQIA+ community. The crossing sits adjacent to [Taylor Square](/wiki/Taylor_Square "Taylor Square"), on the corner of Campbell and Bourke streets, on the border between [Surry Hills](/wiki/Surry_Hills "Surry Hills") and Darlinghurst. First created in 2019,[[20]](#cite_note-2019_Rainbow_Taylor-20) it was updated in 2024 to include the progress pride flag.[[21]](#cite_note-Taylor_2023-21)[[22]](#cite_note-2024_Rainbow_Taylor-22)

The area now known as Darlinghurst lies on the traditional lands of [Gadigal](/wiki/Gadigal "Gadigal") people. They continued to visit and use the place into the 1840s.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

Although close to Sydney, rocky ridges and shallow soil made the area less attractive for the early settlers than other more productive, arable sites and it was not developed by European settlers until the early 19th century. Sandstone was quarried there, originally with [convict labour](/wiki/Convicts_in_Australia "Convicts in Australia"), and it continued to be extracted there into the second half of the nineteenth century, using prisoners from the nearby Darlinghurst Gaol.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

In the 19th century, the prominent ridge of Darlinghurst was the site of [windmills](/wiki/Windmill "Windmill") that made use of the breezes from Sydney Harbour. In about 1819, Thomas Clarkson, a merchant, erected a windmill, built of stone with a rotating top, near what is now the intersection of Liverpool and Darley streets. Close by, two [post mills](/wiki/Post_mill "Post mill") were built. Thomas Hyndes built a fourth mill, close to Caldwell Street. The last of the mills, which reportedly stood until around 1873, was the Craigend mill. It was near [Thomas Mitchell's](/wiki/Thomas_Livingstone_Mitchell "Thomas Livingstone Mitchell") Craigend villa, on the highest point of the ridge, near what is now the top of Beare's Stairs in Caldwell Street.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

The area was originally known as Eastern Hill and then Henrietta Town, after [Governor](/wiki/Governors_of_New_South_Wales "Governors of New South Wales") [Lachlan Macquarie's](/wiki/Lachlan_Macquarie "Lachlan Macquarie") wife, whose second name was Henrietta. The loyalties changed with the change of governors and the suburb became Darlinghurst in honour of [Eliza Darling](/wiki/Eliza,_Lady_Darling "Eliza, Lady Darling"), the popular wife of Governor [Ralph Darling](/wiki/Ralph_Darling "Ralph Darling"), during the early 19th century. The suffix 'hurst' is derived from the Old English word hyrst, meaning wooded area.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

The area became fashionable after Governor Ralph Darling selected the ridge line—from what is now Surrey Street and Tewkesbury Avenue in Darlinghurst, north to the end of [Potts Point](/wiki/Potts_Point "Potts Point")—as an area for homes of members of the colonial elite. Between 1828 and 1831, Darling made seventeen land grants to wealthy merchants, public servants and private citizens. He imposed stringent conditions on development of the land; all plans required his approval prior to construction, all houses had to be £1000 or more in value, and each piece of land could contain only a single residence and had to have a landscaped garden.[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23) Four of the villas were south of what is now William Street, within the Darlinghurst boundary. These were Barham, designed by [John Verge](/wiki/John_Verge "John Verge") for [Edward Deas Thomson](/wiki/Edward_Deas_Thomson "Edward Deas Thomson"); Craigend, designed by and built for [Surveyor General Sir Thomas Mitchell](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Mitchell "Sir Thomas Mitchell"); Rose Hall, the house of [Ambrose Hallen](/wiki/Ambrose_Hallen "Ambrose Hallen") Town Surveyor and Colonial Architect; and Rosebank, likely designed by John Verge and built for Deputy Commissary-General [James Laidley](/wiki/James_Laidley_(administrator) "James Laidley (administrator)"). All four villas survived into the twentieth century, but only Barham survives today, within the grounds of the Sydney Church of England Girls' Grammar School (SCEGGS).[[23]](#cite_note-:0-23)

The area became less fashionable once the Darlighurst Gaol opened in 1841.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Later, the area became partly industrial. A notable factory was that of [Sargent's Pies](/wiki/George_Sargent_(businessman) "George Sargent (businessman)"), in Burton Street, which was producing 150,000 pies a week there, by 1913.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27)

The late 19th century saw Darlinghurst become the final days of English-born Australian novelist [Ethel Pedley](/wiki/Ethel_Pedley "Ethel Pedley"). She died at her home on 6 August 1898 at the age of 39 from complications of cancer whilst writing *[Dot and the Kangaroo](/wiki/Dot_and_the_Kangaroo "Dot and the Kangaroo")*, which would be retrospectively published a year later.

In 1973, Darlinghurst saw a [green ban](/wiki/Green_ban "Green ban") as a ban on all commercial construction was placed by the NSW Builders Labourers Federation (BLF) as residents demanded that all housing should be high density low-rise with adequate provision for low and middle income families to live within the inner-city area.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Darlinghurst_gaol_new_south_wales.jpg/250px-Darlinghurst_gaol_new_south_wales.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darlinghurst_gaol_new_south_wales.jpg)

The Darlinghurst Gaol and the Darlinghurst Courthouse. The gaol is now the site of the [National Art School](/wiki/National_Art_School "National Art School"). 1930

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Sydney_Jewish_Museum_in_Darlinghurst%2C_Sydney.jpg/250px-Sydney_Jewish_Museum_in_Darlinghurst%2C_Sydney.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sydney_Jewish_Museum_in_Darlinghurst,_Sydney.jpg)

Sydney Jewish Museum

Darlinghurst has two of Sydney's museums: the [Australian Museum](/wiki/Australian_Museum "Australian Museum") (a [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history") museum) and the [Sydney Jewish Museum](/wiki/Sydney_Jewish_Museum "Sydney Jewish Museum"). The suburb also features [St Vincent's Hospital](/wiki/St_Vincent%27s_Hospital,_Sydney "St Vincent's Hospital, Sydney"), and is associated with the Sacred Heart Hospice on Darlinghurst Road, and the [Garvan Institute of Medical Research](/wiki/Garvan_Institute_of_Medical_Research "Garvan Institute of Medical Research").

### Darlinghurst Gaol

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Darlinghurst Gaol")]

Main article: [Darlinghurst Gaol](/wiki/Darlinghurst_Gaol "Darlinghurst Gaol")

[Darlinghurst Gaol](/wiki/Darlinghurst_Gaol "Darlinghurst Gaol"), the large sandstone penal complex in the middle of Darlinghurst was built between 1836 and 1840. The large sandstone walls still bear convict markings, and the complex features six wings surrounding a circular chapel. Australian poet [Henry Lawson](/wiki/Henry_Lawson "Henry Lawson") spent time incarcerated here during some of the turbulent years of his life. The last hanging at the jail was in 1907 (Jahn, 1997). The site became [East Sydney Technical College](/wiki/National_Art_School "National Art School") in 1921, but was turned into the [National Art School](/wiki/National_Art_School "National Art School") from 1995.

### Darlinghurst Fire Station

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Darlinghurst Fire Station")]

Main article: [Darlinghurst Fire Station](/wiki/Darlinghurst_Fire_Station "Darlinghurst Fire Station")

[Darlinghurst Fire Station](/wiki/Darlinghurst_Fire_Station "Darlinghurst Fire Station") was completed in 1912, this three-storey brick and stone building occupies a prominent location at the corner of Darlinghurst Road and Victoria Street. It was designed in 1910 by [Walter Liberty Vernon](/wiki/Walter_Liberty_Vernon "Walter Liberty Vernon") (Jahn, 1997). It still functions as a fire station and is listed on the Register of the National Estate.

### Darlinghurst Courthouse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Darlinghurst Courthouse")]

Main article: [Darlinghurst Courthouse](/wiki/Darlinghurst_Courthouse "Darlinghurst Courthouse")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Darlinghurst_Courthouse.jpg/250px-Darlinghurst_Courthouse.jpg)](/wiki/File:Darlinghurst_Courthouse.jpg)

Darlinghurst Courthouse

Darlinghurst Courthouse is an imposing heritage-listed sandstone building on Taylor Square. It was designed by architect [Mortimer Lewis](/wiki/Mortimer_Lewis "Mortimer Lewis") in 1844, and has a Greek Revival style facade. The central block is adapted from an 1823 design in [Peter Nicholson](/wiki/Peter_Nicholson_(architect) "Peter Nicholson (architect)")'s *The New Practical Builder* (Apperly, Irving & Reynolds, 1989).

### Oxford Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Oxford Street")]

Main article: [Oxford Street, Sydney](/wiki/Oxford_Street,_Sydney "Oxford Street, Sydney")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Sydney_in_February_2026_-_41.jpg/250px-Sydney_in_February_2026_-_41.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sydney_in_February_2026_-_41.jpg)

Construction hoardings along Oxford street installed in December 2022 in time for [World Pride 2023](/wiki/World_Pride#Sydney_WorldPride_2023 "World Pride") that was held in Sydney. The hoardings ran down the Darlinghurst section of Oxford street alongside the parade route of the [Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras parade](/wiki/Sydney_Gay_and_Lesbian_Mardi_Gras "Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras") and remained in place until around September 2025 when two thirds of them were removed. 2026[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[Oxford Street](/wiki/Oxford_Street,_Sydney "Oxford Street, Sydney") is the major commercial thoroughfare of Darlinghurst, running from the south-east corner of Hyde Park, through Taylor Square and beyond into Paddington, Woollahra and Bondi Junction, respectively. Oxford Street was originally called The South Head Road and work commenced on the road in 1811 (Faro, 2000). It was a toll road in its early years with the toll gates being located near present-day Glenmore Road. Oxford Street assumed its current name in 1875. Oxford Square is located at the intersection of Oxford and Burton Streets. Oxford Square is also the name of a small shopping centre located opposite, on the corner of Oxford and Riley Streets.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Football Federation Australia](/wiki/Football_Federation_Australia "Football Federation Australia") have their head office at 1 Oxford Street.

### Stanley Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Stanley Street")]

Main article: [Stanley Street, East Sydney](/wiki/Stanley_Street,_East_Sydney "Stanley Street, East Sydney")

[Stanley Street](/wiki/Stanley_Street,_East_Sydney "Stanley Street, East Sydney") is one of the suburb's two secondary restaurant strips (with Victoria Street) and is often referred to as Sydney's first "Little Italy". However, the restaurants range from Japanese, Thai and Italian and the prices range from basic to moderate. There is an annual Italian Festival held here every June.

### Victoria Street

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Victoria Street")]

Victoria Street is the other major cafe strip. Restaurants range from basic to upmarket. The majority of the cafes have outdoor seating. Several iconic restaurants are in this area, such as Tropicana and Bar Coluzzi.

### The Horizon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Horizon")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/AUS_Sydney%2C_Sydney%2C_Forbes_Street_001.jpg/250px-AUS_Sydney%2C_Sydney%2C_Forbes_Street_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:AUS_Sydney,_Sydney,_Forbes_Street_001.jpg)

Horizon Apartments

The [Horizon](/wiki/Horizon_Apartments "Horizon Apartments"), located in Forbes Street, Darlinghurst, is a 43-storey residential high-rise building completed in 1998. It has a distinctive scalloped facade and is finished in rendered concrete. The building was designed by Sydney architect [Harry Seidler](/wiki/Harry_Seidler "Harry Seidler") and is controversial in that it was one of the first high-rise buildings constructed in the predominately low rise area, with critics saying it caused overshadowing of the surrounding area. The Horizon apartments consist of a residential tower and two lower buildings of apartments, 6 levels of car parks, swimming pool, a tennis court, gym and surrounding gardens.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Presbyterian_church_in_Darlinghurst.jpg/250px-Presbyterian_church_in_Darlinghurst.jpg)](/wiki/File:Presbyterian_church_in_Darlinghurst.jpg)

Former Presbyterian Church (1850)

At the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_Australian_census "2021 Australian census"), the population of Darlinghurst was 10,615. 57.9% of people were male and 42.1% female. This was a higher rate of male residents than the national average of 49.3%. In Darlinghurst, 52.7% of people were born in Australia. The most common other countries of birth were England 6.3%, New Zealand 4.0%, Thailand 2.1%, United States of America 2.0% and China 1.6%. 71.8% of people only spoke English at home. Other languages spoken at home included Spanish 2.5%, Thai 2.0%, Mandarin 1.9%, Italian 1.2% and French 1.2%. The most common response for religion was No Religion at 54.3%. Of occupied private dwellings in Darlinghurst, 78.8% were flats or apartments and 19.6% were semi-detached, row or terrace houses, townhouses etc. Just 0.9% of dwellings were separate houses, compared to the national average of 72.2%.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Commercial area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Commercial area")]

Darlinghurst's commercial activity is centred on Oxford Street, and extends to Victoria Street, Flinders Street, Crown Street, William Street, and Darlinghurst Road. There are a large number of cafés, restaurants and take-away food stores throughout Darlinghurst, as well as a large number of pubs and nightclubs, many of which are on Oxford Street. These pubs and clubs were subject to controversial 'lock-out' laws imposed by the Liberal state government in February 2014, in which venues stopped admitting new customers after 01:30, and ceased the service of alcohol between 03:00 and 05:00. These laws were introduced as a response to violence in the Inner City of Sydney that was related to alcohol intoxication. The lockout laws were repealed in January 2020 with a focus on small bars over big nightclubs. There is also a significant retail presence, including fashion retailers.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Transport")]

Darlinghurst is well-served by lots of forms of public transport, with many bus routes from the [Eastern Suburbs](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_(Sydney) "Eastern Suburbs (Sydney)") converging on [Oxford Street](/wiki/Oxford_Street,_Sydney "Oxford Street, Sydney") prior to entering the [Sydney central business district](/wiki/Sydney_central_business_district "Sydney central business district"). [Route 333](/wiki/Sydney_bus_route_333 "Sydney bus route 333") between [Circular Quay](/wiki/Circular_Quay "Circular Quay") and [Bondi Beach](/wiki/Bondi_Beach "Bondi Beach") travels through Darlinghurst along Oxford Street. Routes 352, 373, 396 and 440 also travel through the suburb on Oxford Street. Buses that travel through the centre of Darlinghurst are routes 311 and 389.

There are no train stations in Darlinghurst, however [Kings Cross railway station](/wiki/Kings_Cross_railway_station,_Sydney "Kings Cross railway station, Sydney") on the [Sydney Trains](/wiki/Sydney_Trains "Sydney Trains") [Eastern Suburbs & Illawarra railway line](/wiki/Eastern_Suburbs_%26_Illawarra_railway_line "Eastern Suburbs & Illawarra railway line") is just over the northern border of the suburb. [Museum station](/wiki/Museum_railway_station "Museum railway station") on the [City Circle](/wiki/City_Circle "City Circle"), is located just to the west of Darlinghurst, on the south-west corner of [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney").

## Schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/%281%29_SCEGGS_Performing_Arts_Centre.JPG/250px-%281%29_SCEGGS_Performing_Arts_Centre.JPG)](/wiki/File:(1)_SCEGGS_Performing_Arts_Centre.JPG)

Diana Bowman Performing Arts Centre (SCEGGS)

Darlinghurst Public School, located on the corner of Liverpool Street and Womerah Avenue, was opened in 1884. It teaches both boys and girl students from Kindergarten to Year 6 grade. [Sydney Grammar School](/wiki/Sydney_Grammar_School "Sydney Grammar School") is located on College Street, across the road from [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Sydney "Hyde Park, Sydney") and is a boys-only [private school](/wiki/Private_school "Private school") which opened in 1857.

The Sydney Church of England Girls' Grammar School (now called [SCEGGS Darlinghurst](/wiki/SCEGGS_Darlinghurst "SCEGGS Darlinghurst")) was founded in 1895 in Victoria Street, Darlinghurst. It moved to its current site in Forbes Street, in 1901. The former Marist Brothers High School at 280–296 Liverpool Street is now an apartment complex named Alexandra.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The school occupied this site from 1911 until 1968 when it moved to [Marist College Canberra](/wiki/Marist_College_Canberra "Marist College Canberra").[[33]](#cite_note-33)

The [National Art School](/wiki/National_Art_School "National Art School") of Australia is a specialist art school that started in collaboration with Sydney [TAFE](/wiki/Technical_and_Further_Education "Technical and Further Education") which dates back to 1859. It is now an independant tertiary education provider allowing students to take courses in Fine Art as well offering short courses to the public.[[34]](#cite_note-34) It is located on the former site of the Darlinghurst Gaol and has a gallery which runs art exhibitions throughout the year showing work from students as well as artists from around the world.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

The University of Notre Dame's School of Medicine and the School of Nursing are located in the historic parish buildings associated with the Sacred Heart Parish in Darlinghurst. The complex includes facilities for use by medical and nursing students such as simulated wards, practise wet laboratories and clinical skills laboratories in addition to simulated consulting rooms.

## Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Churches")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Blacket_StJohns_Darlinghust.jpg/250px-Blacket_StJohns_Darlinghust.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blacket_StJohns_Darlinghust.jpg)

St John's Church of England

Darlinghurst has four functioning churches:

* God in the city, an [Assemblies of God](/wiki/Assemblies_of_God "Assemblies of God") congregation affiliated with [Christian City Churches](/wiki/Christian_City_Churches "Christian City Churches")
* St. Pauls Lutheran Church congregation (Sydney Lutheran Parish)
* [St John's](/wiki/St_John%27s_Anglican_Church,_Darlinghurst "St John's Anglican Church, Darlinghurst"), an [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") congregation. St John's operates Rough Edges, a street outreach centre that functions as a cafe and drop-in centre. It provides a range of services to the local community, including the [homeless](/wiki/Homeless "Homeless").
* Sacred Heart Catholic Church is on the corner of Darlinghurst Road and Oxford Street.

There are also a number of other religious organisations in the suburb, including a variety of smaller chapels and religious services attached to [St Vincent's Hospital](/wiki/St_Vincent%27s_Hospital,_Sydney "St Vincent's Hospital, Sydney").

The heritage listed building which previously housed the [First Church of Christ Scientist Sydney](/wiki/Church_of_Christ,_Scientist "Church of Christ, Scientist") is now known as Yirranma Place. It's been repurposed as a work hub for the [Paul Ramsay Foundation](/wiki/Paul_Ramsay "Paul Ramsay") and other nonprofit organisations.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

The former [St Peter's Church, Darlinghurst](/wiki/St_Peter%27s_Church,_Darlinghurst "St Peter's Church, Darlinghurst"), in Bourke Street is now part of [SCEGGS Darlinghurst](/wiki/SCEGGS_Darlinghurst "SCEGGS Darlinghurst") and is known as The Great Hall.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The former rectory in Forbes Street is now a private residence.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Darlinghurst&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Politics")]

The area has often been a battleground between the two councils of the City of Sydney and the [City of South Sydney](/wiki/City_of_South_Sydney "City of South Sydney"). Most of the suburb belonged to South Sydney, however the [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") [State Government](/wiki/Politics_of_Australia "Politics of Australia") moved the borders repeatedly in order to change the make-up of the City of Sydney. Many claim that these shifts have been attempted to shift the balance of power in the favour of the party in control of the State Government, though some residents of Darlinghurst and Potts Point (the former North Ward of the City of South Sydney) felt that the postcodes of 2010 (Darlinghurst and Surry Hills) and 2011 (Elizabeth Bay, Kings Cross, Rushcutters Bay, Woolloomooloo and Potts Point) were relatively neglected by the City of South Sydney.

However, this battle is now moot since both councils were forced by the State Government to [amalgamate](/wiki/Amalgamation_(politics) "Amalgamation (politics)") in February 2004. An election was held on Saturday, 27 March 2004, in order to elect a new council for the redesignated (expanded) City of Sydney.
Critics of the amalgamation have claimed that the election demonstrated strong voter backlash against the State Government for pressing the issue. The [Australian Labor Party](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party "Australian Labor Party"), for whom the area was usually safe, had their primary vote reduced to approximately 20%. The independent [Clover Moore](/wiki/Clover_Moore "Clover Moore") took the [Lord Mayoral](/wiki/Lord_Mayor "Lord Mayor") position, having campaigned against the Government's dismissal of the council.

At a federal level, Darlinghurst falls in the electorates of the [Division of Sydney](/wiki/Division_of_Sydney "Division of Sydney") and Wentworth. Its current parliamentary representatives are [Tanya Plibersek](/wiki/Tanya_Plibersek "Tanya Plibersek") (Labor) and [Allegra Spender](/wiki/Allegra_Spender "Allegra Spender") (Independent). These electorates have some of the state's strongest support for [The Greens](/wiki/Australian_Greens "Australian Greens").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Eternity_Playhouse%2C_Darlinghurst.jpg/250px-Eternity_Playhouse%2C_Darlinghurst.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eternity_Playhouse,_Darlinghurst.jpg)

Eternity Playhouse, Burton Street

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Art_gallery_Darlinghurst_-_Sydney_-_2025.jpg/250px-Art_gallery_Darlinghurst_-_Sydney_-_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Art_gallery_Darlinghurst_-_Sydney_-_2025.jpg)

Rainbow studios art gallery, Liverpool St., Darlinghurst.

Further information: [Culture of Sydney](/wiki/Culture_of_Sydney "Culture of Sydney") and [LGBT history in Australia](/wiki/LGBT_history_in_Australia "LGBT history in Australia")

The Darlinghurst area is famous for the annual [Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras](/wiki/Sydney_Gay_and_Lesbian_Mardi_Gras "Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras"). The parade, first held in 1978 as a protest march by members of the gay and lesbian community, marks the end of a three-week festival and takes place on [Oxford Street](/wiki/Oxford_Street,_Sydney "Oxford Street, Sydney"), the main street that runs through several suburbs, including Darlinghurst.

The [Sydney Jewish Museum](/wiki/Sydney_Jewish_Museum "Sydney Jewish Museum") is on the corner of Darlinghurst Road and Burton Street (148 Darlinghurst Rd) and features exhibitions about the Jewish community in Sydney.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are many small private art galleries in Darlinghurst, including Black Eye Gallery (Darlinghurst Road; photography), King Street Gallery on William (William Street; painting), Conny Dietzschold Gallery (Crown Street; contemporary art), Stanley Street Gallery (Stanley Street; contemporary art), Robin Gibson (Liverpool Street; painting, sculpture), Gallery 9 (Darley Street; contemporary art) and Liverpool Street Gallery (Liverpool Street; contemporary art). Entry to these galleries is free, and most of the exhibitions will display the work of Australian artists.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Taylor_square_substation_and_underground_toilets_-_northern_end_of_Taylor_Square_-_Sydney_2026.jpg/250px-Taylor_square_substation_and_underground_toilets_-_northern_end_of_Taylor_Square_-_Sydney_2026.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taylor_square_substation_and_underground_toilets_-_northern_end_of_Taylor_Square_-_Sydney_2026.jpg)

The [substation and the toilet block](/wiki/Taylor_Square_Substation_No._6_and_Underground_Conveniences "Taylor Square Substation No. 6 and Underground Conveniences") at the northern end of Taylor Square are used by Qtopia for exhibitions and performances related to queer stories in Sydney.

There is also a gallery in the [National Art School](/wiki/National_Art_School "National Art School") that hosts exhibitions in a mid-19th century sandstone building.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

The [Watters Gallery](/wiki/Watters_Gallery "Watters Gallery") in Riley Street was a longstanding institution run by Frank Watters and his associates Geoffrey and Alex Legge, finally closing its doors in November 2018.[[85]](#cite_note-end2018-85)

The [Eternity Playhouse](/wiki/Eternity_Playhouse "Eternity Playhouse") in Burton Street Darlinghurst opened in 2013, following the renovation of the former Burton Street Tabernacle, which was founded in 1887. Following its purchase by the City of Sydney in 2004, plans were gradually developed to renovate the building as a theatre. The 200-seat theatre is the home of the Darlinghurst Theatre Company, and the renovation was designed by architecture firm Tonkin Zulaikha Greer.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In February 2024, a new museum, titled Qtopia, opened in Darlinghurst, celebrating queer stories with an extensive collection of objects related to queer culture in Sydney.[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-87) It sits across three main sites which include the main building at 301 Forbes St., Darlinghurst, the [substation](/wiki/Taylor_Square_Substation_No._6_and_Underground_Conveniences "Taylor Square Substation No. 6 and Underground Conveniences") and the underground [toilet block](/wiki/Taylor_Square_Substation_No._6_and_Underground_Conveniences "Taylor Square Substation No. 6 and Underground Conveniences"), both of which are located at the northern end of Taylor Square.[[88]](#cite_note-Substation_2024-88)[[89]](#cite_note-89)

It initially began as a temporary exhibition held during Sydney World Pride in 2023 and was located in the bandstand pavillion in Green Park, Darlinghurst, as well as at the [National Art School](/wiki/National_Art_School "National Art School") that sits adjacent to Green Park in Darlinghurst.[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91)
The underground toilet block was first built and opened to the public in 1883 until its closure in 1998[[47]](#cite_note-nswshr-1700-47) and it has further been commented that "The public toilet was also a popular beat in the heart of gay Sydney for decades until its closure...".[[88]](#cite_note-Substation_2024-88)
The substations 40-seat theatre hosts performances including music, drag, comedy, cabaret and poetry all year round with numerous performances being held in conjunction with the [Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras](/wiki/Sydney_Gay_and_Lesbian_Mardi_Gras "Sydney Gay and Lesbian Mardi Gras") in February and March each year as well as during [Pride month](/wiki/Pride_Month "Pride Month") in June of each year.[[88]](#cite_note-Substation_2024-88)[[92]](#cite_note-92)
